title: Risotto ai funghi
subtitle: Porcini mushroom risotto with parmesan
excerpt: Dried porcini soaked and their liquid used as stock, combined with fresh chestnut mushrooms, soffrito, white wine, and arborio rice stirred continuously until the grains are swollen and creamy. Finished with cold butter and parmesan. The essential Italian technique for making starchy short-grain rice into something entirely its own.
sourceNotes: Risotto is the definitive rice preparation of northern Italy, particularly Lombardy, Piedmont, and Veneto. The technique (building a creaminess through continuous stirring and the gradual addition of hot liquid, finished with butter and cheese (mantecatura)) developed in its current form in the nineteenth century. Porcini-based risottos are associated with the autumn mushroom season in the Alps and northern Apennines.

content.text: The porcini soaking liquid is the flavour backbone. Pour boiling water over the dried mushrooms and soak for 30 minutes; the liquid turns dark and intensely savoury. When straining, pour slowly and leave the last cloudy tablespoon behind. It contains grit. Top up with chicken or vegetable stock to reach the right liquid volume.

Risotto rice (arborio or carnaroli) is not rinsed. The surface starch is what creates the creaminess as the grains are stirred. Add the stock ladle by ladle, waiting for each addition to be absorbed before adding the next. The final act (mantecatura) is removing the pan from the heat, adding cold butter in pieces, and stirring vigorously until the risotto becomes glossy and flows slowly off the spoon.

items.name: Dried porcini
items.prepNote: soaked in 300 ml boiling water for 30 min, liquid reserved, mushrooms roughly chopped

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: cold, cubed, 30 g for cooking, 30 g for mantecatura

content.text: Sauté the mushrooms and build the base

Heat 30 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} with the {{olive-oil}} in a wide, heavy casserole over high heat. Add the {{mushrooms-chestnut}} and cook without stirring for 4 minutes until golden. Add the soaked {{mushrooms-porcini-dried}} and cook for 2 more minutes. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. In the same pan, cook the {{onion}} for 5 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} and cook for 1 minute.
Toast the rice and add stock

Add the {{arborio-rice}} and stir for 2 minutes until the grains are hot and beginning to look translucent at the edges. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} and stir until fully absorbed. Begin adding the hot {{stock-chicken}} one ladle at a time, stirring constantly and waiting for each addition to absorb before adding the next. Continue for 18 to 20 minutes until the rice is cooked through but still has a very slight bite at the centre. Stir in the cooked mushrooms.

Remove the pan from the heat. Add the remaining cold {{unsalted-butter}} in pieces and most of the {{parmesan}}. Stir vigorously for 1 to 2 minutes until the risotto is glossy and flows slowly off the spoon. It should be loose enough to spread when plated. Rest for 2 minutes. Serve in warm bowls, scatter the remaining parmesan and {{parsley-flat}} over.

items.fix: The rice should still have very slight bite when you start the mantecatura; add a splash of warm stock if stiff; the off-heat rest is essential
items.symptom: Risotto is stiff and gluey rather than creamy and flowing

items.fix: Maintain a steady simmer throughout; keep adding stock one ladle at a time; don't rush by adding two ladles at once
items.cause: Stock added too quickly, or heat too low
items.symptom: Rice grains are still chalky at the centre after 20 minutes

items.fix: Use the strained soaking liquid as part of the stock. It is the most intensely flavoured part; sear the fresh mushrooms at high heat until golden
items.cause: Porcini soaking liquid not used, or mushrooms not sautéed at high heat

items.fix: Warm the bowls; serve immediately; the risotto continues to set as it cools
items.cause: Rested too long or plated on cold bowls
items.symptom: Risotto has dried out by the time it reaches the table

content.text: Risotto ai funghi belongs to the autumn cooking of the Italian Alps and pre-Alps (Lombardy, Piedmont, and the Veneto). The porcini season runs from late August to November, and the rice fields of the Po Valley sit just to the south. The two ingredients meet naturally. The mantecatura step (finishing with cold butter off the heat for a creamy emulsion) is the defining gesture of northern Italian rice cooking. It is the point at which a competent cook becomes a good one.
